name: Emma Cytrynbaum
title: Program Coordinator and Lecturer, Center for Economy and Society
bio: Emma Cytrynbaum is the Program Coordinator for the Center for Economy and Society and a lecturer and advisor in the Moral and Political Economy major. She holds a BA in Political Science from Reed College and a PhD in Political Science from Johns Hopkins University; her research focuses on political theory and the politics of digital media, surveillance, and policing in the United States.

name: John Handel
title: Research Director, Center for Economy and Society
bio: John Handel is a financial and economic historian specializing in historical and contemporary financial market microstructure, back-office financial infrastructure, the relationship between finance and technology, and investment banking, with particular emphasis on nineteenth-century Britain and its global connections. He joins the Center for Economy and Society as Research Director; he was previously Assistant Professor of History at Baylor University, and before that a postdoctoral fellow at the University of Virginia.

name: Trevor Brown
title: Postdoctoral Fellow in Moral and Political Economy
bio: Trevor E. Brown is a postdoctoral fellow in the Center for Economy and Society at Johns Hopkins University, where his research focuses on American politics, political economy, public policy, and the historical causes and consequences of racial, economic, gendered, and place-based inequality. He holds a PhD in Government from Cornell University and a bachelor's degree in Political Science and English Literature from Wittenberg University. He is co-author, with Suzanne Mettler, of Rural Versus Urban: The Growing Divide That Threatens Democracy (Princeton University Press, 2025). In fall 2026 he will join the University of Oregon's Department of Political Science as an Assistant Professor of Inequality.

name: Casey Eilbert
title: Postdoctoral Fellow in Moral and Political Economy
bio: Casey Eilbert is a postdoctoral fellow in the Center for Economy and Society. She holds a Ph.D. in History from Princeton University (2024) and a B.A. in Political & Social Thought from the University of Virginia. Her research focuses on the intellectual history of bureaucracy in modern America, including its ties to political economy, regulation, and the rise and decline of federal state power. Her dissertation, "Conceptualizing 'The Iron Cage': Bureaucracy in Modern America," won the Leo P. Ribuffo Prize for best dissertation in U.S. Intellectual History.

publications.title: Conceptualizing 'The Iron Cage': Bureaucracy in Modern America (dissertation; book project in development)

name: Mustafa Yavas
title: Postdoctoral Fellow, Center for Economy and Society
bio: Mustafa Yavaş is a postdoctoral fellow in the Center for Economy and Society at Johns Hopkins University. He holds a PhD in Sociology from Yale University (2020) and BSc/MSc degrees in Industrial Engineering from Boğaziçi University. His research examines inequality, work and occupations, immigration, social networks, and social theory, approaching economic and political sociology from a global perspective. Before Johns Hopkins, he was a postdoctoral researcher and adjunct lecturer at NYU Abu Dhabi's Division of Social Science.

name: Jacob Roundtree
title: Postdoctoral Fellow, Center for Economy and Society
bio: Jacob Roundtree is a political theorist and Postdoctoral Fellow in the Center for Economy and Society at Johns Hopkins University. He earned his PhD in political theory from Harvard in 2023 and a BA in philosophy, politics, and economics from Colby College in 2010. His research focuses on political epistemology and modern intellectual history, examining how the cognitive and social complexity of capitalism and the modern nation-state bear on the possibility of freedom. His dissertation, a critical genealogy of the social theories of Hegel and Marx, is being revised into a book titled "The Politics of Absolute Freedom." Beyond this core research, he writes on contemporary problems of public ignorance, ideology, propaganda, elite dogmatism, and political polarization as obstacles to democratic rationality. He teaches courses on democracy, truth, Marxism, German Idealism, conservatism, race, and technology, and serves as an assistant editor of the journal Critical Review.

publications.title: Marx's Democratization of Hegel's Philosophy of Right
publications.venue: Critical Review: A Journal of Politics and Society, vol. 33, no. 3-4, pp. 431-461

name: Sanjay Ruparelia
title: Visiting Fellow, SNF Agora Institute
bio: Sanjay Ruparelia holds the inaugural Jarislowsky Democracy Chair at Toronto Metropolitan University, where he is a Professor of Politics and Public Administration. His research examines the politics of democracy, equality, and development in the postcolonial world, with a focus on Indian politics and political economy. He is the author of Divided We Govern: Coalition Politics in Modern India (Hurst & Oxford University Press, 2015), editor of The Indian Ideology: Three Responses to Perry Anderson (Permanent Black, 2015), and co-editor of Understanding India's New Political Economy: A Great Transformation? (Routledge, 2011). His scholarship has appeared in journals including Comparative Politics, Economic & Political Weekly, and Pacific Affairs, and his commentary has appeared in outlets such as the Globe and Mail, Indian Express, and Toronto Star. He co-chairs Participedia, an international research network on democratic innovations, and co-hosts the podcast and lecture series On the Frontlines of Democracy with the Toronto Public Library. He previously taught at the New School for Social Research and Columbia University, and has consulted for the UN Development Programme and the Asia Foundation. He holds a BA from McGill University and an MPhil and PhD from the University of Cambridge.
